citations.narrative:
There are three dogs (one adult male husky, one adult male shepherd/husky mix and one adult female shepherd mix) that
have no names, ID numbers or other forms of identification known by the facility.
Without a means to positively identify individual animals, acquisition, disposition, inventory and medical records cannot be
associated with their respective animals. Being able to tell which animals are mentioned in the records is important to
determine if the animals are being used in accordance with the Animal Welfare Regulations and receiving adequate
veterinary care. The licensee must ensure that all live dogs and cats held on the premises, purchased, or otherwise
acquired, sold or otherwise disposed of, or removed from the premises for delivery or to any person for use as a pet, shall
be identified by a microchip or an official tag of the type described in Section 2.51. The tag may be affixed to the animals'
enclosure, or to their necks by means of a collar made of material generally considered acceptable to pet owners as a
means of identifying their pet dogs or cats, or shall be identified by a distinctive and legible marking acceptable to and
approved by the Administrator.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/30/2026

The licensee does not have acquisition records for three unidentified dogs housed at the facility (one adult male husky,
one adult male shepherd/husky mix and one adult female shepherd mix). According to the licensee and staff, the dogs
were found wandering the property and were reported to the county's animal control department, but are housed in the
area shared with the rest of the animals used for exhibition at the facility, with a sign advertising that they are in need of a
home. Facility representatives clarified that the county said they would come by to get the animals at some point, but it
has been several days and there is no formal, written arrangement between the facility and the county to document that
the facility is operating as an agent of the county or any other municipality.
Records containing the required information for animals disposed of, or acquired for, use in regulated activities (including
those offspring born on premises) are necessary to accurately track animals to ensure their humane care and use. The
licensee must make and maintain records of the acquisition and disposition of all dogs which fully and correctly disclose
the required information on APHIS Forms 2005 and 2006.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/30/2026

Three unidentified dogs housed at the facility (one adult male husky, one adult male shepherd/husky mix and one adult
female shepherd mix) are housed in an outdoor enclosure with a shade covering, but no other form of shelter from the
elements.
Failure to provide dogs with adequate protection from the cold and heat, the direct rays of the sun, and the direct effects
of wind, rain or snow can cause them discomfort, illness or even death. The facility must ensure that all dogs housed
outdoors are provided with one or more shelter structures that are accessible to each animal in each outdoor enclosure,
and that are large enough to allow each animal in the shelter structure to sit, stand, and lie in a normal manner, and to
turn about freely; additionally, shelters in outdoor facilities for dogs must have a wind and rain break at the entrance,
contain a roof, four sides, and a floor, contain clean, dry bedding material if the ambient temperature is below 50 degrees
Fahrenheit, and additional clean, dry bedding if the temperature is 35 degrees Fahrenheit or lower.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/30/2026

During inspection, the inspector requested the written plan for providing exercise for dogs. Although the dogs' current
housing situation qualifies as an acceptable method of providing exercise, the facility did not have this required, written
document available for review during inspection.
Exercise is essential for the good health and well-being of dogs, and a written plan clarifies how this is achieved at the
facility. Dealers, exhibitors, and research facilities must develop, document, and follow an appropriate plan to provide
dogs with the opportunity for exercise. The plan must include written standard procedures to be followed in providing the
opportunity for exercise. In addition, the plan must be approved by the attending veterinarian and be made available
during inspections.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/30/2026

The facility does not have a written Program of Veterinary care that includes three, unidentified dogs housed in an
enclosure along the publicly accessible exhibit pathway (one adult male husky, one adult male shepherd/husky mix and
one adult female shepherd mix).
A written program of veterinary care for dogs is required to ensure that there is an expectation about the care provided to
all dogs. Failure to establish and maintain a written program of veterinary care for dogs can decrease the ability to ensure
they receive adequate veterinary care. A written program of care for dogs must be established and maintained so that the
licensee has a plan for providing adequate preventative care and treatments when needed, including regularly scheduled
visits from the veterinarian, annual exams, and emergency, weekend and holiday care.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/30/2026

The licensee does not have veterinary medical records for the three unidentified dogs housed at the facility (one adult
male husky, one adult male shepherd/husky mix and one adult female shepherd mix).
Veterinary records contain information that is vital for knowing the current health status and preventive veterinary care for
each dog and for detailing the care of animals that have new or pre-existing injuries or illnesses. It is important to have the
veterinary record on hand and up to date, to ensure that caregivers know exactly how and when to provide follow-up care
to animals, including details for preventive and post-procedural care, drug administration and any other instructions and
follow up visits. This written information is very important to ensure that all care provided to the animal is done according
to the veterinarian's orders to help prevent unnecessary discomfort or harm to the animal and help ensure the best
outcome. The licensee must keep updated copies of veterinary records for dogs and make these available for APHIS
inspection; the records must include all of the information detailed in Part 3, Subpart A, Section 3.13(b)(1-4).
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/30/2026

In the enclosure housing seven adult golden pheasants, there are three shelter boxes made of weathered plywood that
have peeling paint and are deteriorated to the point that they are coming apart in many places.
The current state of these surfaces does not allow them to be readily cleaned or sanitized. Inability to clean and/or
sanitize surfaces that birds can contact within their primary enclosure increases the risk of disease transmission to the
animals. The surfaces of housing facilities must be constructed in a manner and made of materials that allow them to be
readily cleaned and/or sanitized, or removed and replaced when worn or soiled.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/30/2026
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by May 22, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
n

3.150(c)(2) 
   
Facilities, general.

species.common: DOMESTIC DUCK INCLUDING ALL DOMESTIC BREEDS
